---
title: "Chapter 2: An Introduction to R"
subtitle: "STA258 — University of Toronto Mississauga"
---

<style>
.lecture-actions {
  display: flex;
  gap: 12px;
  margin: 24px 0 36px 0;
}
.lecture-actions .btn {
  font-size: 1em;
  padding: 10px 22px;
  border-radius: 8px;
}
.summary-card {
  background: #f7f9fa;
  border-left: 6px solid #00695c;
  padding: 18px 22px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  margin: 18px 0;
}
.summary-card h3 {
  margin-top: 0;
  color: #00695c;
}
</style>

<div class="lecture-actions">
<a href="chapter2-slides.html" class="btn btn-outline-secondary" target="_blank">Slides</a>
<a href="chapter2-slides.html?print-pdf" class="btn btn-outline-secondary" target="_blank">PDF</a>
</div>

## What you'll learn

By the end of Chapter 2 you'll be able to:

- Explain why we use **R** rather than point-and-click software, and tell **R** apart from **RStudio**.
- Install R and RStudio and navigate the four main panes of the IDE.
- Use R as a **calculator** — arithmetic, exponents, logs, and built-in math functions.
- Create and work with the core R **data structures**: vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames.
- Call built-in **functions** like `mean()`, `sd()`, `hist()` and read their output.
- Install and load **packages** with `install.packages()` and `library()`.

## Key ideas

::: {.summary-card}
### R vs RStudio
**R** is the engine that runs the calculations. **RStudio** is the dashboard you use to drive it. You install R first, then RStudio on top of it.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Scripts beat point-and-click
Writing analysis as code makes it **reproducible**, easy to share, and easy to re-run when the data changes. This is the single biggest reason statisticians use R.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### R as a calculator
R handles `+ - * /`, exponentiation `^`, modulo `%%`, integer division `%/%`, and a full set of math functions (`sqrt`, `log`, `exp`, `abs`, `round`) and trig functions out of the box.
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Data structures
- **Vectors** — same-type values in one dimension
- **Lists** — mixed types, any structure
- **Matrices / Arrays** — same type, 2+ dimensions
- **Factors** — categorical variables with named levels
- **Data frames** — table of variables (columns) and observations (rows)
:::

::: {.summary-card}
### Functions and packages
Most R commands look like `function_name(input)`. **Packages** extend R with new functions — install once with `install.packages()`, load each session with `library()`.
:::

## How to use this chapter

- Open the **slides** for an interactive walkthrough — every code block in the deck is editable and runs in your browser.
- Use the **PDF** button for an offline-friendly version.
- Try the in-slide exercises (mean, histogram). Change values, re-run, and check the output.
